abstract:
This paper addresses the critical challenge of detecting false data injection attacks (FDIAs) within smart grid infrastructure by reformulating attack detection as a statistical learning problem. Traditionally, such attacks are identified using state vector estimation (SVE) methods that analyze measurement residuals; however, these techniques often fail to detect "unobservable" attacks where malicious data is injected into the column space of the measurement Jacobian matrix. To overcome these limitations, this study explores a various machine learning frameworks—including supervised, semi-supervised, and online learning—to classify measurements as either secure or compromised.

The proposed approach utilizes several prominent algorithms, such as Support Vector Machines (SVM), $k$-Nearest Neighbors, and Sparse Logistic Regression, integrated with decision- and feature-level fusion techniques (e.g., AdaBoost and Multiple Kernel Learning). By analyzing the geometric and statistical properties of measurement vectors, the researchers demonstrate that machine learning models can effectively identify unobservable attacks that blind traditional SVE methods. Experimental evaluations conducted on IEEE 9-bus, 57-bus, and 118-bus test systems reveal that these learning-based methods achieve superior detection performance, precision, and recall. Notably, the study identifies a phase transition in SVM performance that correlates with the theoretical minimum number of sensors an attacker must compromise to remain unobservable. The findings highlight the potential of both batch and real-time online learning algorithms to enhance the resilience and security of smart grid physical layers.
